title: Container network not reachable - Recv failure: Connection reset by peer
body:
### Issue Description

Some containers can't be connected to via http://localhost:<port> and yet others are. I initially thought I had a bug in my code that I was testing, but am able to reproduce with other containers. Confusingly, though, some images will consistently work and yet others consistently fail. I'm yet to discern what they're doing differently!

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue

1.

   ```sh
   $ podman run -d --rm --p 8001:8001 docker.io/library/python:3.13.7-slim-trixie python -m http.server -b 0.0.0.0 8001
   c18a8e002d73bad7a7356340b250459d96586e8653bcee83cff97c93f7f15f90
   $ curl http://localhost:8001
   curl: (56) Recv failure: Connection reset by peer
   ```
2.

   ```sh
   $ podman run -d --rm -p 8080:80 docker.io/library/nginx:latest
   8b828548ebd460420d93eef1ac81c3f1847e55eb0e75cb262c899e45b54642aa
   $ curl http://localhost:8080
   <!DOCTYPE html>
   <html>
   <head>
   ...
   ```



### Describe the results you received

1. The first test fails to connect to the server despite ports being published
2. The second test succeeds, even though the command line args are the same aside from the image used.

### Describe the results you expected

I'd expect both tests to succeed

### podman info output

```yaml

  - io
  - memory

    version: 'conmon version 2.1.13, commit: '
  cpuUtilization:
    idlePercent: 96.75
    systemPercent: 0.79
    userPercent: 2.46
  cpus: 8
  databaseBackend: sqlite
  distribution:
    distribution: fedora
    variant: workstation
    version: "42"
  eventLogger: journald
  freeLocks: 2037
  hostname: kyoto.local

    - container_id: 0
      host_id: 1000
      size: 1

      host_id: 524288
      size: 65536
    uidmap:
    - container_id: 0
      host_id: 1000
      size: 1
    - container_id: 1
      host_id: 524288
      size: 65536
  kernel: 6.16.7-200.fc42.x86_64
  linkmode: dynamic
  logDriver: journald
  memFree: 5370413056
  memTotal: 16513347584
  networkBackend: netavark
  networkBackendInfo:
    backend: netavark

      package: aardvark-dns-1.16.0-1.fc42.x86_64

    version: |-
      crun version 1.24
      commit: 54693209039e5e04cbe3c8b1cd5fe2301219f0a1
      rundir: /run/user/1000/crun
      spec: 1.0.0
      +SYSTEMD +SELINUX +APPARMOR +CAP +SECCOMP +EBPF +CRIU +LIBKRUN +WASM:wasmedge +YAJL
  os: linux

    executable: /usr/bin/pasta
```

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

No

### Additional environment details

Stock latest Fedora 42 packages in use:

* `podman-5:5.6.1-1.fc42.x86_64`
* `passt-0:0^20250911.g6cbcccc-1.fc42.x86_64`

I've also tried downgrading to the previous versions in case it's a recent regression but these packages also exhibit the same problem:

* `podman-5:5.4.1-1.fc42.x86_64`
* `passt-0:0^20250320.g32f6212-2.fc42.x86_64`

### Additional information

_No response_

comments.body:
I don't know how my previous googling didn't turn up https://github.com/containers/podman/discussions/25497 before.

This is from the host:

```sh
$ cat /etc/hosts
# Loopback entries; do not change.
# For historical reasons, localhost precedes localhost.localdomain:
127.0.0.1   localhost localhost.localdomain localhost4 localhost4.localdomain4
::1         localhost localhost.localdomain localhost6 localhost6.localdomain6
# See hosts(5) for proper format and other examples:
# 192.168.1.10 foo.example.org foo
# 192.168.1.13 bar.example.org bar

$ getent hosts localhost
getent hosts localhost
::1             localhost localhost.localdomain localhost6 localhost6.localdomain6
```

So when I `curl http://localhost:8080` it's resolving to an ipv6 address which my axum server wasn't listening on, and nor, obviously, is the test python container in the original bug report.

`curl http://127.0.0.1:3000` works, as expected. Changing the axum server to bind to `:::3000` as per the above discussion post makes `curl http://localhost` work too.

> So when I `curl http://localhost:8080` it's resolving to an ipv6 address which my axum server wasn't listening on, and nor, obviously, is the test python container in the original bug report.
> 
> `curl http://127.0.0.1:3000` works, as expected. Changing the axum server to bind to `:::3000` as per the above discussion post makes `curl http://localhost` work too.

Given this, do you think there's anything more that Podman or pasta could do to prevent this kind of issue?

The only solution I see would be pasta silently deciding to remap requests to 127.0.0.1 to ::1 and back, and we plan to add a some kind of configuration interface, eventually, that would allow configuring this.

But I guess it's not desirable to make that the default, so others might hit the same exact issue. Anything else you have in mind?

> Given this, do you think there's anything more that Podman or pasta could do to prevent this kind of issue?

tl;dr - I don't think so. However...

So, what caused me so much confusion was the fact that I _wasn't_ getting a connection refused, but instead a connection reset. So that suggested to me that the server _was_ listening on the right IP/port combo but somehow misbehaving.

Given what I know now, it still confuses/surprises me somewhat. If the server binds _only_ to an IPv4 address but a client tries to reach it via an IPv6 address then I'd _naively_ expect it to fail with a connection refused error. However, I also understand that there may well be some IPv6 -> IPv4 translation/proxying/magic going on that might explain the behaviour I saw. Certainly, if I'd have got a connection refused error I likely would have gone looking for what `localhost` was actually pointing to much sooner than I did. However, I suspect making things behave like that is also likely to completely break other setups given IPv6 vs IPv4 compatibility stuff.

Thanks for responding, by the way. Happy for you to close it if you don't think there's much improvement possible.

> > Given this, do you think there's anything more that Podman or pasta could do to prevent this kind of issue?
> 
> tl;dr - I don't think so. However...
> 
> So, what caused me so much confusion was the fact that I _wasn't_ getting a connection refused, but instead a connection reset. So that suggested to me that the server _was_ listening on the right IP/port combo but somehow misbehaving.

Oops, now I see, we have another case of https://github.com/containers/podman/discussions/26767#discussioncomment-14353320 / https://bugs.passt.top/show_bug.cgi?id=131 (tagging @dgibson).

Long story short, right now pasta can only accept the connection and reset it right away if a given port is forwarded but nobody is listening.

That's because we call `listen()` on the corresponding socket, and the kernel always completes a handshake for us before we even call `accept()` on the connection.

We could change this, though, with some periodic probing feature, and not have an inbound listening socket at all if we see that nobody inside the container is listening.

> However, I also understand that there may well be some IPv6 -> IPv4 translation/proxying/magic going on that might explain the behaviour I saw.

This is actually independent from the IP version.

> Certainly, if I'd have got a connection refused error I likely would have gone looking for what `localhost` was actually pointing to much sooner than I did.

Right, I see how that's confusing.

> Happy for you to close it if you don't think there's much improvement possible.

We can improve this, so let me add a pointer from https://bugs.passt.top/show_bug.cgi?id=131 to here and keep this one open to track the missing feature in Podman itself: even once we add that functionality in pasta, we'll still need some form of Podman integration here.

Instead of closing the socket, can pasta try IPv4 instead? Wouldn't proxying to IPv4 work as well as proxying to IPv6?
This is what curl does - try IPv4 if IPv6 doesn't listen. So it will be consistent. If this is remembered - even better so that next requests will go directly to IPv4.

Also this will restore compatibility with slirp4netns.

> Instead of closing the socket, can pasta try IPv4 instead?

If that's not explicitly configured by the user, that would look like a giant security hole to me...

> Wouldn't proxying to IPv4 work as well as proxying to IPv6?

Yes, we could add that *as an option*, eventually, but it's different from:

> This is what curl does - try IPv4 if IPv6 doesn't listen.

...this. It's not curl doing that, it's the resolver: you connect to localhost which resolves to both ::1 and 127.0.0.1.

pasta, instead, doesn't resolve anything: it sees a packet going to ::1 and it should make it go to 127.0.0.1 instead, for whatever reason.

> Also this will restore compatibility with slirp4netns.

That (automatically proxying IPv6 to IPv4) looks like a bug to me. A convenient one, but still a bit worrying. Still, it might make sense as an option. Let's see what we can do for that in terms of https://pad.passt.top/p/NewForwardingModel.

> If that's not explicitly configured by the user, that would look like a giant security hole to me...
> ...
> That (automatically proxying IPv6 to IPv4) looks like a bug to me. A convenient one, but still a bit worrying. Still, it might make sense as an option.

Why would connecting to IPv4 endpoint while client accessed IPv6 be a giant security hole while connecting IPv6 while client accessed IPv4 on host is fine? Because this is the [existing behavior](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/22959#issuecomment-2318736143).  I fail to see any security threat here and nobody previously called slirp4netns' behavior a security issue.

When one publishes ports, automatically both IP versions get exposed (and user cannot choose between them). This means the intended and user expected behavior is just to expose a port in the container whatever the underlying IP version is.

> It's not curl doing that, it's the resolver: you connect to localhost which resolves to both ::1 and 127.0.0.1.

While there are technical differences here, I think the underlying logic is quiet the same.

```
curl -v "http://api-2.staging.apicast.dev.localhost:8080/?user_key=***"
* Host api-2.staging.apicast.dev.localhost:8080 was resolved.
* IPv6: ::1
* IPv4: 127.0.0.1
*   Trying [::1]:8080...
* connect to ::1 port 8080 from ::1 port 52114 failed: Connection refused
*   Trying 127.0.0.1:8080...
* Connected to api-2.staging.apicast.dev.localhost (127.0.0.1) port 8080
...
```

So the hostname was resolved to both, then the client tried first one, then the other.

In the case of podman and it's networking (be it pasta or something else), the user just published a port (`-p 8080:8080`). The user did not specify IP version (and also cannot specify). That means user expects to access whatever port is available within the container, the same way as user connects to a hostname that may be accessible through both IP versions.

As for the use case, it is very common that in local development one wants to access things through `something.localhost` and that since a long time is first resolved to IPv6. Many programs bind to IPv4 because of portability to platforms where IPv6 is unavailable. That makes setting up stuff locally often too complicated and error prone when using pasta networking.

> > If that's not explicitly configured by the user, that would look like a giant security hole to me...
> > ...
> > That (automatically proxying IPv6 to IPv4) looks like a bug to me. A convenient one, but still a bit worrying. Still, it might make sense as an option.
> 
> Why would connecting to IPv4 endpoint while client accessed IPv6 be a giant security hole while connecting IPv6 while client accessed IPv4 on host is fine? Because this is the [existing behavior](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/22959#issuecomment-2318736143).

I remember your table, and I was suggesting you'd file a separate ticket for the bit of inconsistent behaviour you've seen. I just filed https://bugs.passt.top/show_bug.cgi?id=152.

But you only tested connections to loopback addresses (127.0.0.0/8 or ::1) there, which are not so much of an issue in this sense: what the container sees is a connection from loopback, and there IPv4 or IPv6 doesn't matter so much.

And I have to admit that your table and **that** inconsistent behaviour is actually more relevant here than what I'm mentioning next, but still, let me show you the issue with a generic IPv4 <-> IPv6 translation approach.

The problem arises when we do this with non-loopback addresses. Let me pick local addresses:

 * server in container listens to IPv4, client connects to IPv4, pasta maps both: the connection works
    ```
    $ pasta --config-net -t 31337 -- socat -T1 TCP4-LISTEN:31337,accept-timeout=2 OPEN:/dev/null & { sleep 1; socat EXEC:'echo x' TCP:88.198.0.164:31337; }
    $ 
    ```
 * server in container listens to IPv4, client connects to IPv6, pasta maps both: the connection fails
    ```
    $ pasta --config-net -t 31337 -- socat -T1 TCP4-LISTEN:31337,accept-timeout=2 OPEN:/dev/null & { sleep 1; socat EXEC:'echo x' TCP:[2a01:4f8:222:904::2]:31337; }
    $ 2025/09/25 18:27:41 socat[1] W accept: Connection timed out
    ```
    * server in container listens to IPv6, client connects to IPv4, pasta maps both: the connection fails
    ```
    $ pasta --config-net -t 31337 -- socat -T1 TCP6-LISTEN:31337,accept-timeout=2,ipv6only OPEN:/dev/null & { sleep 1; socat EXEC:'echo x' TCP:88.198.0.164:31337; }
    $ 2025/09/25 18:27:58 socat[1] W accept: Connection timed out
    ```
    * server in container listens to IPv6, client connects to IPv6, pasta maps both: the connection works
    ```
    $ pasta --config-net -t 31337 -- socat -T1 TCP6-LISTEN:31337,accept-timeout=2,ipv6only OPEN:/dev/null & { sleep 1; socat EXEC:'echo x' TCP:[2a01:4f8:222:904::2]:31337; }
    $ 
    ```

> I fail to see any security threat here and nobody previously called slirp4netns' behavior a security issue.

If we generically mapped between IP versions as needed / convenient, changing the source addresses, I would argue that would be a mild case of [CVE-2021-20199](https://www.cve.org/CVERecord?id=CVE-2021-20199), which was very much called a security issue (of rootlessport, actually, not slirp4netns itself) because applications in the container might be configured to apply different security policies depending on the source address, or to listen on a particular one.

If it's loopback, I guess you're right, it's just "loopback", whatever the IP version is.

> When one publishes ports, automatically both IP versions get exposed (and user cannot choose between them).

Actually, you can choose between them, by binding explicitly to ::1 or to 127.0.0.1.

> This means the intended and user expected behavior is just to expose a port in the container whatever the underlying IP version is.

I... hope it actually is the expected behaviour. I can see myself in two months answering a ticket where the user has an application binding to 127.0.0.1 and we just did the most outrageous thing ever by mapping an IPv6 loopback connection to it. Well, at least I have somebody else to blame. :)

> > It's not curl doing that, it's the resolver: you connect to localhost which resolves to both ::1 and 127.0.0.1.
> 
> While there are technical differences here, I think the underlying logic is quiet the same.
> 
> ```
> curl -v "http://api-2.staging.apicast.dev.localhost:8080/?user_key=***"
> * Host api-2.staging.apicast.dev.localhost:8080 was resolved.
> * IPv6: ::1
> * IPv4: 127.0.0.1
> *   Trying [::1]:8080...
> * connect to ::1 port 8080 from ::1 port 52114 failed: Connection refused
> *   Trying 127.0.0.1:8080...
> * Connected to api-2.staging.apicast.dev.localhost (127.0.0.1) port 8080
> ...
> ```
> 
> So the hostname was resolved to both, then the client tried first one, then the other.

Right, but if the hostname didn't resolve it to both, curl wouldn't do it on its own. This is a substantial difference with what you expect from pasta (and it's fine, but... in general applications don't do that).

> That makes setting up stuff locally often too complicated and error prone when using pasta networking.

Fair point. That's https://bugs.passt.top/show_bug.cgi?id=152 now. I'll report back here (and on https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/22959, which is partially overlapping but different) once it's fixed.

> > This means the intended and user expected behavior is just to expose a port in the container whatever the underlying IP version is.
> 
> I... hope it actually is the expected behaviour. I can see myself in two months answering a ticket where the user has an application binding to 127.0.0.1 and we just did the most outrageous thing ever by mapping an IPv6 loopback connection to it. Well, at least I have somebody else to blame. :)

I'm not really convinced this is the user expected behaviour.  I mean, obviously it is in this case, but I can really imagine other cases where people would be baffled by automatically forward between IP versions without an explicit request to.  In particular, I think it would be really inadvisable to do this automatic forwarding for non-spliced connections - and I think an inconsistency between spliced and non-spliced behaviour is at least as likely to trip people up as not automatically forwarding does in this case.

Perhaps more to the point, I think we can get the desired behaviour for this case without doing so.  The inconsistent behaviour here is certainly a bug.  If we forwarded only 127.0.0.1 -> 127.0.0.1 and ::1 -> ::1, things should work as expected *if* the IP families that passt listens on matches those that the guest listens on.  In that case the client itself may fall back, and we don't need to guess at exactly how and whether it will do so.

So, it's a matter (again) of making the host-side listen()s match the guest side listens.  That can be done manually now (as @sbrivio-rh pointed out, you can publish either 127.0.0.1 or ::1 explicitly).  We can do it automatically when we implement listening port probing, which we want to do anyway.

I definitely agree to not map between protocol versions and the fact the rootlessport does it is is an implementation detail and not something worth aiming for again because it breaks the pure ipv6 use case (https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/14709). I am looking forward to get rid of rootlessport with podman 6.0 if we are pasta only then.


> So, it's a matter (again) of making the host-side listen()s match the guest side listens.

Yes but also what is important that the port must still be bound on the host on startup to ensure you can only start one container on the given host port and not have race conditions where several containers are started on the same port and then at runtime pasta fails to bind. As bind() and listen() are two separate calls I assume that should still be possible, I only mention it so that side isn't overlooked.

> > So, it's a matter (again) of making the host-side listen()s match the guest side listens.
> 
> Yes but also what is important that the port must still be bound on the host on startup to ensure you can only start one container on the given host port and not have race conditions where several containers are started on the same port and then at runtime pasta fails to bind. As bind() and listen() are two separate calls I assume that should still be possible, I only mention it so that side isn't overlooked.

Right.  Conflicting bind()s should conflict, whether or not either one is listen()ing.  I hadn't initially considered separating the bind() from the listen().  Keeping around sockets bound, but not listen()ing for ports we've probed as not available might be useful.  I _think_ that will give the same result to a peer trying to connect as no socket at all.

We still have a potential race when our probe tells us to turn off a listen().  There's no unlisten(), so we have to close the socket, then make and bind() a new one when we need it again.

My tentative plan for the port probing was to listen() initially for all ports we might forward, then close them again when/if our probes to the guest fail.  There are going to be some races whichever way we do it, but I think listening initially will give us better results. Specifically it avoids the case where you start a container which *is* listening on the port, then start a client, but the client fails because pasta needs a moment to complete the probe and start listening.

> We still have a potential race when our probe tells us to turn off a listen(). There's no unlisten(), so we have to close the socket, then make and bind() a new one when we need it again.

Ah right, that sounds somewhat problematic from a reliability standpoint though. What would happen if the port bind fails? I guess for most "server" applications that bind/listen once on startup it will be fine but the applications that bind/close new ports more dynamically it introduces new failure points that are quite hard to debug as it would be a race condition.

I would suggest, whatever the solution is, to get a docker compatible behavior *by default* as bad as it sounds. Other behavior can be configurable. And this can be documented in `man podman run`. 

Everybody uses docker, also very popular with the different NASes but people doing their own infra are few and far in between. Using podman is becoming tiring as we have no negotiation power to push projects to adapt to our preferences. As another example see handling of health checks, see #18904 / #25454

P.S. @sbrivio-rh and thank you for filing the pasta issue, I didn't even realize it had another bug tracker. I'm just a user and don't know what depends on which component.

> I would suggest, whatever the solution is, to get a docker compatible behavior by default as bad as it sounds. 

Did you try to run docker rootless? Because comparing rootful networking vs rootless networking is not fair, we try our best to hide such details but they work fundamentally different. Ultimately a rootless way involves two sockets (one on the host ns and one in the container ns) so preventing there is only one can only go so far.

AFAIK rootless docker uses rootlesskit which can also be configured to use pasta for example so I would think it behaves the same or very similar in this regard?

> > > This means the intended and user expected behavior is just to expose a port in the container whatever the underlying IP version is.
> >
> > I... hope it actually is the expected behaviour. I can see myself in two months answering a ticket where the user has an application binding to 127.0.0.1 and we just did the most outrageous thing ever by mapping an IPv6 loopback connection to it. Well, at least I have somebody else to blame. :)
> 
> I'm not really convinced this is the user expected behaviour. I mean, obviously it is in this case

And perhaps for the original reporter of https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/22959, plus 2-3 other cases reported in the past that I can't find right now.

But maybe they would all be fixed by making the behaviour consistent as you mention later, plus all the fixes for issues that came up while checking https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/26798 (in particular, local mode for IPv4 and IPv6 separately, see https://bugs.passt.top/show_bug.cgi?id=128).

> but I can really imagine other cases where people would be baffled by automatically forward between IP versions without an explicit request to. In particular, I think it would be really inadvisable to do this automatic forwarding for non-spliced connections - and I think an inconsistency between spliced and non-spliced behaviour is at least as likely to trip people up as not automatically forwarding does in this case.

The main reason which makes this acceptable to me, though, is a given interpretation of "loopback".

That is, if instead of forwarding connections originally going to ::1 or 127.0.0.1, we think of the current "spliced" implementation as something that just forwards between loopback interfaces (main peculiarity over the tap data path), then we could just see this as making our best effort to connect loopback interfaces, abstracting away which particular IP version implements this.

On the other hand:

- the Linux kernel gives you the possibility to bind a specific loopback address, so it's not necessarily something one can abstract. What if you have different processes in the container, listening, separately, to 127.0.0.1 and ::1? What would we forward ::1 to?

- even worse: what would we forward 127.0.0.53 to? See https://bugs.passt.top/show_bug.cgi?id=113. To fix that, we'll need to preserve specific loopback destination addresses (for IPv6 it's only one, for IPv4 it's a whole /8 block), and once we do that, it would look quite unnatural if we mangled addresses in a general case

- I wasn't aware of https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/14709 that @Luap99 just linked. Oops. Well, I guess one could do `podman run --net=pasta,--ipv6-only ...`, but that's even less natural

> Perhaps more to the point, I think we can get the desired behaviour for this case without doing so. The inconsistent behaviour here is certainly a bug. If we forwarded only 127.0.0.1 -> 127.0.0.1 and ::1 -> ::1, things should work as expected _if_ the IP families that passt listens on matches those that the guest listens on. In that case the client itself may fall back, and we don't need to guess at exactly how and whether it will do so.

@akostadinov, @mattburgess (I quote) [i]If we forwarded only 127.0.0.1 -> 127.0.0.1 and ::1 -> ::1, with pasta not listening at all on the IP version the container is not listening to, would things work in the cases you presented? If they do, this is clearly a better solution.

> AFAIK rootless docker uses rootlesskit which can also be configured to use pasta for example so I would think it behaves the same or very similar in this regard?

Right, it's rootlesskit, see https://docs.docker.com/engine/security/rootless/troubleshoot/#networking-errors, so it should be exactly the same: you can use rootlessport / slirp4netns, and get the IPv4 / IPv6 cross-mapping, or pasta, and exactly the same behaviour as you'd get with Podman. By the way note that IPv6 with pasta on Docker was generally not functional until a while ago, I fixed that in https://github.com/rootless-containers/rootlesskit/pull/458.

> Using podman is becoming tiring as we have no negotiation power to push projects to adapt to our preferences. As another example see handling of health checks, see https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/18904 / https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/25454

But in that case it looks like there would be two solutions, that is, either 1. extend the OCI specification and implement whatever health check thing Docker implements, or 2. ...just implement it. I don't see any strong feeling against it. It's just that you or somebody else gets to implement it.

Same here, nobody is breaking things on purpose for the sake of correctness. It's just that if we fix some we break some others, so if the 1:1 strict forwarding actually fixes things for (most?) users and doesn't break other users, let's go for it.

Look at all the thumbs down on https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/14709. Not that I really understand the point of those (maybe just implement the damn thing instead of complaining? I think (almost) to myself...), but that's a symptom we would break things for some users.

> P.S. [@sbrivio-rh](https://github.com/sbrivio-rh) and thank you for filing the pasta issue, I didn't even realize it had another bug tracker. I'm just a user and don't know what depends on which component.

Both trackers are fine, really. Filing passt tickets give us a bit more focus but nobody is picky about that. If it comes from a Podman user, it can also just be tracked here.

I haven't tried docker rootless. The point IMO is to strive for compatibility in default behavior because projects just make things work by default on docker. And at some point becomes too much to maintain as well risks of unexpected side effect increases.

> I haven't tried docker rootless. The point IMO is to strive for compatibility in default behavior because projects just make things work by default on docker. And at some point becomes too much to maintain as well risks of unexpected side effect increases.

Strive, sure, but some things just aren't possible without root.  Others are possible only with complex trickery.

The point here was about rootless Docker. In general, we can take care of every aspect mentioned here as rootless.

And of course we should make things work on Podman just like they do on Docker. In this case, we definitely can.

The only remaining point here in my view is whether the proposed solution, that is:

> @akostadinov, @mattburgess (I quote) [i]If we forwarded only 127.0.0.1 -> 127.0.0.1 and ::1 -> ::1, with pasta not listening at all on the IP version the container is not listening to, would things work in the cases you presented? If they do, this is clearly a better solution.

would actually work.

If that does (waiting for confirmation from @akostadinov and @mattburgess), let's go for it.

If it doesn't, we'll need to find some other compromise.

> If we forwarded only 127.0.0.1 -> 127.0.0.1 and ::1 -> ::1, with pasta not listening at all on the IP version the container is not listening to, would things work in the cases you presented?

That certainly sounds like it would for my case. That should give me the connection refused error back which would make me look at DNS/hosts.

Thanks!

Wouldn't that be actually harder, given that pasta can try the port but if the service is not yet ready, that might be a false signal that pasta should not listen to that port?

Either way, I'm convinced that given no standard syntax to specify whether the target container port is IPv4 or IPv6, the expected behavior is to access any of them. When one specifies `--publish`, local IP can be specified but not container IP/protocol.

If one wants to expose to the world, then they will specify host listening IP and setup service access controls (if any) accordingly.

So for me _"pasta not listening at all on the IP version the container is not listening to"_ would be error prone and most likely unexpected for the user. But will be much better behavior that current situation and probably will cover most of the use cases.

IMHO a warning about potential security configuration implications with port forwarding to the Internet can live in the man page.

PS also consider that such change will be a breaking change for the services listening on IPv6 and are accessible both ways right now, but that will stop working after the change. But again, anything but current behavior would be much better and probably work OOB in most scenarios.

PPS if this IPv4<->IPv4 and IPV6<->IPv6 is implemented, please add an option to enable the cross-forwarding. It is definitely useful for local development to just be able to access a service on a port and not care what kind of localhost the client app is seeing.
